name: Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22)
name_original: Castillo de Almonacid de Toledo – circular por el sendero Virgen de la Oliva (PR-TO 22)
description: Hike the moderate 8.2-mile Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22) near Toledo, Spain, past olive groves, Almonacid Castle, and church ruins.
region_intro: Hiking around Almonacid De Toledo offers routes through a landscape characterized by expansive plains and extensive olive groves in the Guazalete river valley, part of the wider Tajo river valley. A prominent hill crowned by a historic medieval castle defines the area's elevation. The terrain includes flat, open sections and gentle ascents, often passing by ancient structures like a Roman bridge. Best hiking trails around Almonacid De Toledo The most popular hiking route is Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22), an 8.2 miles (13.2 km) moderate trail that takes 3 hours 36 minutes to complete, winding through olive groves and past the Almonacid Castle. Another top favourite among local hikers is Sierra de Nambroca Trail, a moderate 6.3 miles (10.1 km) path. This route offers views of the surrounding plains and takes approximately 2 hours 53 minutes. Local hikers also love the Castro Reservoir Trail, a 3.1 miles (5.0 km) trail leading through the Guazalete river valley, often completed in about 1 hour 18 minutes. Hiking around Almonacid De Toledo is defined by olive groves, expansive plains, and the historic castle, with routes often following the Guazalete river valley. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to moderate trails. The routes in Almonacid De Toledo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.8 stars from around 10 reviews. More than 100 hikers have used komoot to explore Almonacid De Toledo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Almonacid De Toledo?
A: There are over 25 hiking trails around Almonacid De Toledo, offering a variety of options for different skill levels. You'll find everything from easy walks to more moderate routes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes around Almonacid De Toledo?
A: The landscape around Almonacid De Toledo is characterized by expansive plains and extensive olive groves, particularly within the Guazalete river valley. You'll encounter flat, open sections, gentle ascents, and routes that pass by ancient structures like a Roman bridge. The prominent hill crowned by the historic castle also defines some of the area's elevation.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Almonacid De Toledo?
A: Autumn is particularly recommended for hiking in Almonacid De Toledo, offering pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery, especially along trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1018783543/virgen-de-la-oliva-trail-pr-to-22" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22)&lt;/a&gt;. Spring also offers mild weather and blooming landscapes.

Q: Are there easy hiking options for beginners or casual walkers?
A: Yes, Almonacid De Toledo offers 8 easy hiking routes. An excellent option for a casual walk is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021167294/castro-reservoir-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Reservoir Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is 3.1 miles (5.0 km) long and leads through the Guazalete river valley.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Almonacid De Toledo?
A: Yes, several routes in the area are circular. A notable example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1018783543/virgen-de-la-oliva-trail-pr-to-22" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22)&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 12.5-kilometer circular path winding through olive groves and past the Almonacid Castle.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural features can I see while hiking?
A: Many trails integrate with the region's rich history and natural beauty. You can expect to see the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2127963" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almonacid Castle&lt;/a&gt; perched atop a hill, ancient structures like a Roman bridge, and the 16th-century Ermita de Nuestra Señora de la Oliva. The area also offers excellent birdwatching opportunities, especially for raptors and steppe birds.

Q: Are there any castles to explore near the hiking trails?
A: Absolutely. The most prominent is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2127963" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almonacid Castle&lt;/a&gt;, which is a central attraction and often part of hiking itineraries. Other nearby castles include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2210456" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of the Counts of Orgaz&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3792122" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñas Negras Castle&lt;/a&gt;, which can be reached via routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021150409/castillo-de-penas-negras-from-mora" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Peñas Negras from Mora&lt;/a&gt; trail.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Almonacid De Toledo?
A: The trails in Almonacid De Toledo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.8 stars from around 10 reviews. Hikers often appreciate the blend of historical sites and natural landscapes, particularly the olive groves and views from the castle.

Q: Are the hiking trails in Almonacid De Toledo suitable for families with children?
A: Many of the easier routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021167294/castro-reservoir-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Reservoir Trail&lt;/a&gt;, are suitable for families. The generally gentle terrain through olive groves and plains makes for accessible walks, though some routes may have moderate sections.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many of the natural trails in the Almonacid De Toledo area. However, it's always advisable to keep them on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas or near wildlife, and to check for any specific local regulations on individual routes.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trails in Almonacid De Toledo?
A: Parking is typically available in or near the village of Almonacid De Toledo, and at trailheads for popular routes. For specific trails, it's recommended to check local signage or online resources for designated parking areas.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay overnight in Almonacid De Toledo?
A: Almonacid De Toledo, as a municipality, offers local amenities including places to eat and accommodation options. These can provide a convenient base for exploring the hiking trails and the surrounding region.
tips: From any point approaching Almonacid de Toledo, and from dozens of kilometers away, the castle's silhouette can be seen perched on a promontory, as if still guarding the Camino de La Mancha. It is a fine example of a 14th-century castle, intended as a permanent residence for troops and as a military settlement for surveillance and defense.
At the foot of the hill where the castle is located are the ruins of the old Renaissance church of Almonacid that will form part of the enclosure that included the old castle.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to hike the Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22)?
faqs.answer: The trail is approximately 13.2 kilometers long and generally takes around 3 hours and 36 minutes to complete. This duration can vary depending on your pace and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: Is the Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22) suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Classified as a moderate hike, the trail is accessible for hikers with a reasonable fitness level. It features a relatively gentle elevation change of about 193 meters, making it suitable for those new to hiking who are looking for a bit of a challenge without being overly strenuous.

faqs.question: Is the route a loop trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22) is designed as a circular route, meaning you will end up back at your starting point without having to retrace your steps.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: While specific trailhead parking details are not provided, the trail is located near municipalities like Almonacid de Toledo, Nambroca, and Orgaz. It's advisable to check local parking options in these towns, particularly in Almonacid de Toledo, which is often associated with the start of the route.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail traverses varied landscapes typical of central Spain. You can expect to walk through agricultural areas, including picturesque olive groves, and possibly some natural spaces. The elevation changes are moderate, ranging from a low of around 661 meters to a high of 811 meters above sea level.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: Along the way, you'll encounter historical and natural sights. Notable points of interest include the impressive Almonacid Castle and some charming Church ruins. The name 'Virgen de la Oliva' itself suggests a connection to the abundant olive trees that are a common feature of the Spanish landscape.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike in this region of Spain is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for hiking, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Is the Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22) dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, trails in this region are dog-friendly, but it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas or near livestock. Ensure you carry enough water for both yourself and your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for the trail?
faqs.answer: As a 'Sendero de Pequeño Recorrido' (PR) or short-distance trail, the Virgen de la Oliva Trail (PR-TO 22) typically does not require permits or charge entrance fees. These trails are generally open to the public for recreational use.

faqs.question: Does the trail connect with other hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Sendero Virgen de la Oliva and the Camino de Santiago del Sureste, offering glimpses of other established walking paths in the region.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or amenities nearby?
faqs.answer: The trail is located near several municipalities, including Almonacid de Toledo, Nambroca, and Orgaz. These towns would be the best places to find amenities such as cafes, restaurants, and shops before or after your hike.
